messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Map the project to the call's expected outcomes. Provide quantified KPIs and pathways to impact.
Write the Expected outcomes and impacts section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.1 Credibility of the impacts

How the activities proposed support the development of a business model and the product features: Our R&D methodology is application focussed, whereby we will develop the Visum LSR focussing on the PVC recycling application, which was the subject of the predecessor H2020 REMADYL project. We were very fortunate in view of the commercial potential of our innovation to have been selected by the Horizon Results Booster programme, which provided us with expert advice and mentorship in the development of an initial Business Plan for the LIBS PVC sorting system. We will continue this work in the EIC Transition project, whereby over two distinct project phases we have planned activities for analysing market potential and performing market segmentation (with the assistance of our 19 global distributors), ensuring regulatory compliance in a ‘by design’ approach (cooperation with UNE), continually monitoring the competition and technology landscape in parallel with FTO analysis and the implementation of an IP strategy to secure IP assets to protect and strengthen our commercial opportunity. All of these activities will shape an up-to-date business model design (underpinned by a strong Value Proposition) process and investor-ready business planning exercise. Tied into this process will be the refinement and finalisation of the product’s features, which will be informed by a cocreation (with ≥40 early adopters and other stakeholders), validation, and market engagement process implemented over two complete innovation cycles, so that the LIBS-RS system evolves to meet both commercial and technical demands with the goal of ensuring its success in the marketplace. In turn, the product-related features and ability to implement the inspection system in two different scenarios in relation to the applications will inform our business model development, whereby as was seen static spot will be suited for single and non-conveyed samples (such as the ones from mining prospection to be inspected in an at-line setup), whereas dynamic spot will be suited for moving samples (such as raw materials or products on a conveyor belt which are typical in the recycling sector).
How the activities support the commercialisation and other relevant aspects (intellectual property rights, regulation, certification and standardisation): Our project activities integrate industry and market feedback, business strategy, technical validation, and regulatory compliance, creating a solid foundation for the successful commercialisation of the LIBS-RS system to ensure the system is market-ready, legally compliant, and attractive to a range of industries. While our market entry application segment is plastics recycling (leveraging the PVC use case), we aim to quickly move into adjacent segments in the online waste recycling industry (Table 1), as well as prepare for other industrial applications (real-time monitoring in the process industries, environmental monitoring, among others), which will be analysed based on our market research and consultation process with our network of 19 European and international distributors covering 27 countries and a wide range of industrial segments.
Fundamental to this is the creation of a comprehensive business and financing plan, which will integrate a market feasibility analysis, cost-benefit assessments, and a clear IP strategy. The identification of market demand, ensuring the financial viability of the product, regulatory costs, and potential returns on investment, in addition to a plan for scaling up the technology post-project will prepare us for a fluid Time to Market (TTM)- which we estimate could be within 24-36 months based on previous experiences of scaling up and accelerating spectroscopy-based devices to market- and the commercialisation process.
In parallel, the product development will be deeply linked to the broader commercialisation efforts. Cooperation with industry from the PVC production and recycling space (Deceuninck, Veolia, PreZero, INEOS), a sorter OEM (PICVISA), the wider process industries (A.SPIRE), Regional Development Agency (ACCIO) and standardisation bodies (UNE), among other stakeholders,  provides a cocreation opportunity for developing a dual spectroscopic fusion analyser that is unique in the competition landscape. This co-creation and industrial validation of the functional prototype will provide critical proof of concept, demonstrating its capability in real-world environments, with a by-design approach to obtaining the necessary certifications and meeting industry-specific standards. Deploying the system in industrial settings will highlight its effectiveness, generating the data needed to navigate regulatory requirements and move towards certification.
IRIS will execute a strong IP strategy (discussed below), protecting the innovations with IPR tools- utility models, patents, trade secrets, trademarks- as assets for competitive advantage, which could also open potential licensing opportunities to expand the system’s market reach in the future. In parallel, the team will map out the regulatory and certification landscape, ensuring the product meets all necessary legal and industry standards, which not only supports compliance but also strengthens the system’s market credibility, making it more attractive to customers and partners alike. As an opto-electronic inspection system intended for industrial deployment, the device will be designed to comply with CE marking directives, including the Low Voltage Directive (LVD), Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC), and the Machinery Directive where applicable. In parallel, harmonised standards such as IEC 61010 (safety requirements for electrical equipment for measurement, control, and laboratory use) and ISO 9001 for quality management will guide the system’s engineering and production. Moreover, the AI components will be developed in line with the EU AI Act, integrating Trustworthy AI principles such as transparency, robustness, and human oversight. Early engagement with standardisation bodies (UNE, with LOI secured) will ensure alignment with emerging technical standards, particularly in spectral instrumentation and waste sorting systems, and will allow the VISUM LRS to anticipate the certification pathways necessary for market entry.
Strategy for IP management: Our strategy for the management of intellectual property (IP) to ensure Freedom to Operate (FTO) and to adequately protect our results as relevant to ensure the commercial exploitation of the project.
Firstly, IRIS confirms it owns the background from the REMADYL project that is being used in this EIC Transition project, i.e. the prototype, knowledge and knowhow related to the development of an identification and sorting system based on LIBS technology integrated in a continuous recycling line processing PVC waste. No Background IPR is needed from any Third Parties.
In relation to the proposed development in this EIC Transition project, whereby we will develop a LIBS-Raman dual spectroscopic fusion analyser that leverages common hardware in an optical fusion, and uses hybrid emsembling for spectral data fusion, we have conducted a first patentability study (using patent libraries- EPO and WIPO) to identify potential IPR issues with existing technologies & patents. We have established our FTO and differentiation points. Our IP strategy is to protect this development with IPR tools (patent and/or utility model), and we are engaged with our patent attorney’s ClarkeModet to commence preparations for IP registration. We will also register the Visum LRS as a trademark (we already have trademarked the VISUM brand in Europe and USA). We will also assess other IPR tools, such as design rights and trade secrets. All IP assets will be owned by IRIS Technology Solutions, and will support exploitation by protecting our innovation, providing competitive market exclusivity, attracting investment, enabling licensing opportunities, and strengthen brand identity, all of which drive business growth and market success.
Market potential: In the REMADYL project, the IRIS innovation (‘Prototype allowing for the sorting of PVC waste according to the presence or concentration of Lead’) was selected as the Key Exploitable Result (KER) for benefiting from the Business Plan Development services of the Horizon Results Booster programme. As such, with expert advice of the BPD services team, including mentoring and specialised workshops, we performed initial exploration of potential markets. In addition, we engaged with one of the most important manufacturers and recyclers of PVC window frames in Europe, Deceuninck, to define industrial requirements,  and to test and validate the prototype and demand. We also assessed the offering in the competition landscape and positioned our differential value. Key findings and updates are summarised below (focussed on our market entry PVC recycling use case):
■ Market entry segment /early adopters: Plastics recycling segment, targeting early adopters from the PVC recycling use case, i.e. Recyclers and PVC-based product manufacturers who recycle PVC containing lead stabilizers in order to re-process and build new PVC products containing limited amounts (<0.1%) of lead. Region: Spain (domestic market) and Europe (well networked with flagship early adopters, which makes this viable, and we also have a European distribution network currently covering Spain, Portugal, Germany, Poland, Ukraine, France, Belgium, Switzerland, Turkey, Greece, Slovakia, Czech Rep).
■ Unmet problem: With the new regulations entering into force, these businesses must ensure PVC does not contain 0.1% of lead (as well as other restricted LS). Currently, they do not have access to technically and economically viable solutions for recycling PVC according to its lead content, and these businesses are at risk of non-compliance.
■ Market size: Within the global plastics recycling market that was valued at US$ 46.8 Billion in 2024, and predicted to reach US$ 101.6 Billion by the year 2034, at a 8.1% CAGR, the PVC recycling market was valued at approximately US$ 3.3 billion in 2023 and is expected to grow to US$ 4.9 billion by 2028, at a CAGR of 8.0% (MarketsandMarkets, GlobeNewswire). This is a compelling use case to target, as an affordable dual spectroscopy fusion analyser for detecting the concentration of both lead stabilisers (LIBS) and plasticisers (RS) in the one system would represent a significant leap forward for PVC recycling, and would give us an ‘unfair advantage in the market’.
■ Market segmentation: For our PVC recycling use case, the market can be further segmented by Stabiliser Type use, whereby we will only target recyclers and manufacturers processing PVC with Pb stabilisers. Furthermore, old PVC constitutes most of current hard (e.g. window frames, tubes) and soft (e.g. flooring or cables) post-consumer PVC waste. As such, companies recycling (and reprocessing) these types of products will be the main target, and to further segment our market entry strategy, we could prioritise PVC recyclers/producers of window frames and tubes. In the EIC Transition, we will expand the market analysis and segmentation to map out additional segments that will inform our GTM. As discussed, we aim to quickly move into adjacent segments in the online waste recycling industry (Table 1), as well as prepare for other industrial applications (real-time monitoring in the process industries, environmental monitoring, among others).
■ SOM: For the market entry use case, IRIS will tackle a 1% of the total PVC recycling market (to be updated in EIC Transition market analysis work in WP3 and WP6). Driver: The system ensures compliance with European regulations: EU's Restriction of Hazardous Substances (RoHS) directive restricts lead and its compounds in all electrical and electronic products. The limit is 0.1%. The EU has expanded the restriction of lead to PVC articles under entry 63 to Annex XVII of REACH. The new law will become effective on November 29, 2024. The latest amendment restricts lead to < 0.1% in PVC polymer and copolymer articles.
■ Competitors: Key players such as TOMRA and PICVISA dominate the large-scale industrial sorting market. Their solutions are built around traditional optical and spectroscopic technologies (e.g. NIR, RGB, XRT, and in the case of TOMRA’s AUTOSORT™ PULSE, conventional LIBS). However, none currently offer an integrated LIBS–Raman hybrid inspection system capable of detecting low-concentration contaminants such as lead stabilisers and phthalates with the required sensitivity for compliance with EU regulations. However, they are also potential OEM partners as part of our GTM (Go to market) strategy. They provide optical large-scale sorting solutions that can sort at high speeds and can process high amounts of feedstock. A LOI from PICVISA will allow us to explore partnership OEM licensing opportunities. Discussions are underway to explore a technology licensing model whereby VISUM LRS could be integrated into PICVISA’s existing sorting platforms to enhance their capabilities. On the sensor front, Lasertechnik Berlin’s CORALIS system is one of the few examples of an integrated LIBS–Raman analyser. However, it is a laboratory-grade instrument, limited to static, small-area sampling due to its compact XYZ stage and Raman microscopy setup. CORALIS is not designed for real-time, high-throughput industrial environments and lacks the optomechanical and AI-driven integration needed for dynamic inline sorting. Precisely IRIS’s market positioning is in bridging the critical gap between laboratory-grade spectroscopic instruments and industrial-scale sorting performance. The VISUM LRS is a category-defining innovation that combines detection sensitivity, integration readiness, and AI intelligence to deliver the next generation of Process Analytical Technology (PAT) for real-time materials inspection.
■ Willingness to Pay (WTP): Positive, as the sorting technology promotes sustainability, circularity and re-use of lead, and importantly is an enabler for regulatory compliance. It is timely. If we consider the ambitious targets of VinylPlus 2030, which is the current 10-year Commitment of the European PVC industry to Sustainable Development, to achieve ≥1 million tonnes/year of recycled PVC used in new products by 2030, and to foster science-based solutions for the safe and sustainable use of additives (such as innovative sorting technology), the VISUM LRS clearly addresses a recognised and urgent market demand. It will help recyclers in future-proofing their operations, reducing risk, and unlocking premium markets for compliant recycled PVC. There are 2 key drivers: the need (compliance) & the lack of low-cost alternatives to satisfy this need. This is an optimum scenario.
Validation of problem/solution fit: A very robust approach for validating the product/solution fit of the LIBS-RS system is through our participatory cocreation process (requirements and specifications scoping with ≥ 40 industry early adopters and stakeholders- e.g. Deceuninck, Veolia, PreZero, INEOS, PICVISA, UNE, SPIRE, ACCIO), as well as performing a pilot deployment in an industrial PVC processing and recycling facility (Deceuninck in Belgium) where the system’s ability to detect and sort contaminated PVC waste can be directly tested in real-world operational settings. Deceuninck is a very credible early adopter to be engaged with, as designer, developer and distributer of cutting-edge window, door and building profiles, with a deep commitment to sustainability, and who now face strict regulatory requirements for hazardous material detection. They are ideally placed to validate both the technical and economic value of the solution. During the pilot, we will gather quantitative data on sorting accuracy, speed, and efficiency, along with qualitative feedback on ease of integration into existing workflows. The validation process will allow us to measure KPIs such as compliance with lead detection regulations (below 0.1%), ability to detect plasticisers and other LS, to validate the system's reliability, and the impact on overall operational efficiency. Thanks to the two innovation loops that will be performed in the project, we will be able to incorporate Deceuninck’s feedback into the iterative development process, and also inform the post-project upscaling roadmap based on insights from operators, which will help refine the product features, as well as from the business level in relation to how well the LIBS-RS system addresses their pain points—such as the need for rapid, real-time monitoring and the capability to sort materials based on lead content, investment costs, operational savings, ROI, removal of risk of non-compliance, etc., which will inform Business Case Studies for wide dissemination. ■ Addressing CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost): Our go-to-market strategy is designed to minimise CAC through deep early adopter engagement, co-development with an industrial pilot site, and strong OEM and distributor alignment. Through our LOI with PICVISA (a leading OEM in sorting and recycling equipment), we gain access to an existing sales and support infrastructure, reducing the need for costly direct sales. Our global network of 19 reputable and experienced distributors covering 27 countries across Europe, Latin America, Asia and North Africa, and who already serve relevant industrial customers, further lowers customer acquisition effort and enables faster penetration across target segments. They are also well positioned and understand their industrial markets and are a very credible route for problem/market validation. By demonstrating regulatory compliance and cost savings during the pilot (e.g. reduced rejection rates, less reprocessing, lower regulatory risk), we will build a compelling ROI case that supports rapid adoption across existing sales channels.
Planned measures to maximise the impact of the project: We have prepared an initial ‘Plan for the dissemination and exploitation, including communication activities’ (hereafter DEC Plan), which is discussed below:
Communication Activities: Our communication strategy is underpinned by a clear definition of our target groups, communication goals, key messages, and tools & channels to be used (Table 4). Overarching goals are to: 1) generate awareness about the VISUM LRS among our EU and international channels & industry clients, research/academic partners, stakeholders (industry associations, clusters, regional and national development agencies, policy makers), our employees and the general public; 2)  mobilise the engagement and participation of key industry decision makers and stakeholders over the course of the project.
Table 4- Target groups of our communication strategy
Our experienced marketing department will lead the design and implementation of our communication strategy, which will be supported with a suite of communication tools and content, among them:
Branding. VisumLRS will form part of trademarked VisumTM brand (positioned for tech leadership, innovation, service, reliability).
Website. Incorporate dedicated pages in our corporate website to present the project; our SEM/SEO strategy (identifying and optimising the most suitable key words for increasing visitor traffic, which will be monitored & analysed to obtain visitor insights).
Content strategy. Include Visum LRS in our content strategy, producing high quality blog content that will be published on our website and pushed out via our established social media channels (e.g. LinkedIn >18,000 followers).
Communication materials (print, digital and audiovisual): flyers, roll up and banners (displayed in our offices- exposure among our employees, visiting clients and partners, and used at tradeshows/exhibitions), videos (effective short clips: showcasing the technology, its applications & benefits/presenting our participation in EIC Transition/interviews with key researchers or engineers/interviews with early adopters, etc.).
Exhibitions and Tradeshows: participation in ≥3 tradeshows/exhibitions, e.g. International Fair of Recovery and Recycling (SRR); The Plastics Recycling Show Europe, etc.
Publications: ≥3 commercial publications in online/offline magazines and blogs.
PR strategy: well-timed and prepared Press Releases to coincide with key development milestones and outputs (issued through relevant press channels, including the European Commission News Section).
Dissemination of the Project Results: In line with an Open Science Strategy, we will ensure the early and wide dissemination or public disclosure of all the non-confidential project results, via appropriate means, including scientific publications. The main dissemination tools, among others, that we will use are:
Scientific publications (≥2) in relevant Open Access journals- e.g. Recycling; Resources, Conservation & Recycling Advances (RCR Advances); etc.; Participate in ≥3 international exhibitions and conferences- oral/poster/papers, e.g. International Fair of Recovery and Recycling; Share results on online media (research data, software, reports); Organise ≥2 training and workshop sessions; Demo Days or Hosting Individual Companies to showcase the development to ≥40 prospective users/customers over the course of the project.
Standardisation activities: IRIS will engage with UNE (representing CEN-CENELEC in Spain), with whom we have active cooperation (in 2024 we received the UNE Award for the Integration of Standardisation and Innovation). We will meet with UNE in the early stages of the project (and thereafter every 6 months), to update our review of existing standards, ensuring compatibility with market conditions and increasing transparency for prospective customers, and to assess potential to contribute to standards.
Exploitation: The main goal of our exploitation plan is to make use of the results of the project for post-project scale up, for a swift TTM so that we can bring to market a brand new high-performance dual spectroscopic fusion analyser (Visum LRS). To date no off-the shelf similar industrial-grade LIBS-Raman PAT device exists, paving an opportunity to be first to market and open the door to numerous real-time monitoring and inspection applications in waste recycling, mining, (bio)pharmaceuticals, environmental monitoring, as well as in health, agriculture, and defence. The exploitation plan will be completed in line with the Technology Scale Up Roadmap (produced in WP5) and the Business Model & Business Plan development activities (WP3, WP6).

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Map the project to the call's expected outcomes. Provide quantified KPIs and pathways to impact.
Write the Expected outcomes and impacts section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.1 Our project’s pathways towards impact

a) Unique contribution of our project results to (1) the outcomes specified in this topic, and (2) the wider impacts, in the longer term, specified in the destinations in the work programme

We make advanced photonics and multimodal sensing more accessible as key enabling technologies for Europe’s sustainable, digital, and sovereign future. We deliver affordable and easy-to-use sensing that combines precision, versatility, and intelligent integration to improve efficiency, reliability, and decision-making in industry, while cutting resource use and operational costs. Thanks to strengthening the innovation of European technology players from the photonics supply chain (ICL/QCL- ALPES, SPAD imaging- NV (a newcomer to Horizon Europe), photonics integrator- IRIS), we make a solid contribution to strengthening Europe’s open strategic autonomy by reducing dependence on external suppliers in critical sensing technologies. By giving industries (such as bioprocessing- MyB, plastics recycling- VW, and steelmaking- CELSA) access to new multimodal tools for increasing their efficiencies and sustainability, thanks to actionable, reliable data, information, and predictions, we support compliance, competitiveness, and resilience across important European industrial sectors, directly contributing to the green and digital transitions.
(1) Contribution to the Expected Outcomes: We will make a tangible contribution to the expected outcomes specified in the HORIZON-CL4-2025-04-DIGITAL-EMERGING-01 call topic, as follows:
Increase the efficiency of developed photonic sensors, surpassing existing technologies in aspects such as energy consumption, data acquisition and processing speed, as well as measurement accuracy: We improve efficiency at both module and system level. The compact ICL/QCL MIR module is expected to cut power consumption by ~30–40% compared to current laboratory-scale MIR systems, while maintaining or extending spectral coverage. The SERS module will deliver up to 10× enhancement in sensitivity versus conventional SERS, reducing acquisition times from seconds to sub-second levels. The SPAD camera with on-chip processing can reduce data transfer and processing latency by 50–70%, enabling real-time analysis at multi-kHz frame rates. By fusing modalities at the physics and signal level, we reduce redundant measurements and increase effective information yield per photon captured, leading to a projected 20–30% improvement in accuracy, repeatability, and robustness in complex industrial environments. Together, these advances represent step changes over existing bulky, energy-intensive, and single-purpose photonic systems.
Significantly reduce the size, weight, or footprint of the sensors and quantify advancements over current technologies: We tackle the footprint challenge through chip-level integration, modular co-design, and miniaturisation. The compact QCL source reduces MIR sensing modules from benchtop units (~20–30 kg, >0.5 m² footprint) to portable modules under 5 kg and <0.05 m². The SPAD camera with on-chip processing eliminates the need for external processing racks, reducing system weight by ~60% and footprint by ~70%. SERS modules built on nanostructured substrates reduce the need for bulky optics, cutting package size by ~50%. By co-integrating sensing heads and enabling shared sampling geometries, we project an overall system-level reduction of ~40–60% in size and weight compared to current multimodal setups that combine discrete instruments. These reductions will be validated in the three pilots, where industrial-grade TRL6 (ending in TRL7) modules will replace current benchtop instruments. This miniaturisation not only enables easier integration into industrial lines (steel, plastics, bioprocessing) but also opens pathways to portable, field-deployable, and scalable sensing platforms for additional domains (food, environment, security).
Improve both intrinsic and extrinsic performance metrics to boost measurement accuracy for at least three use-case scenarios: We boost both intrinsic (sensitivity, limit of detection, selectivity) and extrinsic (robustness, stability, applicability in harsh conditions) performance across the 3 industrial pilots:
■ Bioprocessing (pharma): MIR ATR + CSA + SERS + SPAD fusion is expected to lower LoD for critical metabolites and the target antibiotic (oxytetracycline) by 1 order of magnitude (µM → sub-µM or nM range), improve measurement repeatability by ~20%, and cut analysis time from minutes to <30s. SPAD adds visual inspection in SWIR (1100 nm) for macroscopic biofilm/heterogeneity detection, enabling more reliable real-time bioprocess control.
■ Steelmaking: CSA+ MIR + Raman + SPAD fusion characterises slag composition, off-gases, and fume contaminants with ~30% improved precision compared to single techniques, while operating robustly at >1,200°C. Extrinsic robustness will be validated with stable uptime >95% under harsh EAF conditions, and SPAD imaging adds anomaly detection for alloy charge control.
■ Plastics recycling: MIR + CSA + SERS + LIBS fusion enables classification and contaminant detection, improving polymer sorting accuracy from ~85% (current SoA) to >95% and reducing false positives by ~40%. LIBS contributes heavy metal and bromine/cadmium detection, while SERS enhances trace detection of PFAS and stabilisers.
Table 10 – Projected Our Performance Gains Across Use Cases
These quantifiable gains show that we can significantly outperform single-modality sensing systems in real-world industrial conditions, where both intrinsic measurement fidelity and extrinsic robustness are critical for adoption.
Extend the sensing and testing capabilities of photonic sensors by linking optical with non-optical measurement parameters (e.g. for acoustic sensing or electromagnetic sensing). We extend the scope of photonic sensors by fusing optical modalities (MIR, SERS, SPAD imaging, Raman/HSI, LIBS) with non-optical parameters captured through chemical sensor arrays (CSA)/electronic noses (VOC profiles, gas composition), as well as through auxiliary channels such as temperature, acoustic vibration, or electromagnetic interference. This linkage allows the system to capture multi-physics & multi-chemical signatures that single photonic modalities cannot resolve entirely on their own.
In biopharmaceutical processing (UC1), combining MIR (ATR) and SERS spectra with CSA profiles of volatile by-products strengthens monitoring of culture states, enabling early detection of contamination, metabolic shifts, and product formation kinetics that purely optical sensors would miss.
In plastics recycling (UC2), MIR and SERS identification of polymers and additives linked with CSA detection of VOCs captures both material composition and contaminant emissions, improving classification accuracy, compliance with REACH/POP limits, and real-time sorting efficiency.
In steelmaking (UC3), MIR/chemosensors off-gas analysis fused with Raman slag chemistry and SPAD imaging of molten dynamics provides both solid and gaseous process signatures, improving furnace control, early contaminant detection, and safety margins.
The result is a broader and more integrated detection envelope, covering chemical, physical, and environmental parameters in one configurable platform. We project a 25-40% increase in detectable parameter space and up to 30% higher measurement robustness compared to photonic-only systems, giving operators a holistic, real-time process view essential for adoption in complex industrial environments.
Contribute to reducing manufacturing costs and increasing resource efficiency, while also enhancing reliability and durability of the targeted sensor systems: We contribute to reducing manufacturing costs and increasing resource efficiency, while also enhancing reliability and durability of the targeted sensor systems: We replace multiple discrete instruments with a single configurable multimodal platform. Deploying MIR, SERS/Raman, LIBS, SPAD, and chemical sensor arrays today often requires 3–5 standalone devices with a combined cost exceeding €300k; we are projected to be able to deliver equivalent or superior capability at 30–40% reduction in system-level costs.
Compact QCL/ICL sources and SPAD modules with on-chip processing reduce component count by ~25–35%, while shared optics, common gas-sampling interfaces, and physics-level fusion lower material demand by ~20%. Industrial-grade packaging, ruggedised optics, and SSbD-informed material choices target uptime >95% in harsh environments (vs ~80–85% for lab-based systems). In addition, self-calibration routines and cross-modal drift correction reduce recalibration needs by ~30% and extend mean time between failure (MTBF) beyond 10,000 hours.
This combination of lower capital expenditure, reduced material intensity, and improved lifetime performance ensures that we deliver resource-efficient, durable, and cost-effective sensor systems, fully aligned with EU sustainability and circularity objectives.
Help secure the open strategic autonomy for Europe by ensuring intellectual property and production means of key technologies are maintained within the EU, reducing dependencies and enhancing negotiation power in technological cooperation. We are structured to keep core IP and manufacturing inside the EU across photonics and sensing. Key hardware KERs, i.e. compact MIR QCL modules (ALPES), SERS modules, InGaAs SPAD camera with on-chip processing (NV), rugged photonic packaging (HS), and our multimodal platform (IRIS), are all developed and industrialised by EU-based SMEs with EU supply chains for critical subcomponents wherever feasible. ICP will lead an EU-first IP strategy (patents, trade secrets, defensive publications), while TEMA will work with our consortium in designing EU manufacturing/business models. UNE will support by early definition of standards to ensure interoperability. Data and software assets follow EU data sovereignty principles (EU hosting options, export-controlled modules ring-fenced, OSS where appropriate with EU-friendly licenses). This will result in reduced dependency on non-EU vendors, stronger EU bargaining position in tech cooperation, and faster industrial uptake within Europe.
Regarding intellectual property, the EC has highlighted IPR as crucial

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Map the project to the call's expected outcomes. Provide quantified KPIs and pathways to impact.
Write the Expected outcomes and impacts section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.1 Our project’s pathways towards impact

a) Unique contribution of our project results to (1) the outcomes specified in this topic, and (2) the wider impacts, in the longer term, specified in the destinations in the work programme

(1) Contribution to the Expected Outcomes: We will make a tangible contribution to all five expected outcomes specified in the HORIZON-CL4-INDUSTRY-2025-01-TWIN-TRANSITION-37 call topic, as follows:
We will validate innovative carbon-neutral iron and steel making solutions within a system-level approach and in consideration of diverse materials with varying quality (raw input materials & reductants mix) & energy needs.
We validate scalable, carbon-neutral iron and steelmaking solutions through a truly integrated systems-level approach, addressing the interdependencies between carbon neutral reductants, recycled materials, optimized energy use, digital intelligence, and human operations in real-world industrial contexts. Steelmaking is a complex system, where variations in feedstock composition, reductant quality, and process conditions cascade through the production chain, influencing thermodynamics, kinetics, slag chemistry, emissions, and ultimately, product quality. The transition to low-CO₂ steelmaking further heightens this complexity by introducing more heterogeneous, bio-based and waste-derived inputs, such as bio-anthracite, process gas and green hydrogen, which challenge the stability and predictability of traditional processes. To address these heightened challenges, we integrate innovations across five interdependent system levels- feedstock mixing, process circularity, energy integration, digital intelligence and workforce capability- ensuring that the system as a whole remains stable, efficient, and capable of delivering high-quality steel under decarbonised operating conditions.
On the materials side, we convert diverse, underutilised into high-quality bioanthracite & green hydrogen gas, obtaining up to 70% biogenic carbon recovery. These alternative reductants are validated as substitutes for fossil-based coke, coal, and natural gas across all major steelmaking pathways (DRI–EAF, BF–BOF, EAF) and in SiMn alloy production, notably silicomanganese using C–SiO₂-rich bioanthracite & catalytic slag valorisation.
On the circular process side, we incorporate two novel slag reuse pathways: (1) using ferroalloy slag as a catalyst in methane cracking to produce hydrogen and carbon-rich residues for SiMn production, and (2) co-compacting EAF slag and metal powder with bioanthracite to produce high-density, stable reductant briquettes for injection in EAFs, reducing dust and enhancing foaming/carburising behaviour.
On the energy side, a comprehensive modelling activity integrates pilot data to identify waste heat reuse pathways, optimise integrated energy balances, and quantify the benefits of integrated biogenic routes. The modelling will also inform cost-performance comparisons between conventional and bio-based steelmaking systems.
On the digital level, HoloSteel, our modular digital intelligence layer, combines PATs, QbD, and a holonic multi-agent digital twin to deliver Industry4.0 grade real-time monitoring, predictive simulation, and adaptive decision support. This enables steel plants to maintain process stability, transparency, and flexibility, even under fluctuating feedstock quality and conditions.
On the human and organisational side, we co-develop new roles, training materials, and decision-support tools for operators and managers. These tools will be tested during our project and scaled post-project, ensuring workforce readiness for the digital and green transition in steel in alignment with Industry 5.0 principles.
By validating these innovations as a coherent, interoperable system, we will show that full-system decarbonisation of steelmaking is achievable without compromising quality, economic viability, or operability. Scaled to just 15% of EU steel plants by 2050, we could enable direct annual GHG reductions of up to 24 Mt CO₂, supporting the Clean Steel Partnership roadmap, Net-Zero Industry Act, and broader EU Green Deal objectives.
Address high-risk factors at macroscopic and microscopic level through detailed characterisation of the physical and chemical interactions that could compromise the optimal functioning of the processes
We systematically address high-risk factors that could compromise the reliable functioning of low-carbon steelmaking processes. This is done both at the macroscopic system level (e.g., process stability, route integration, energy efficiency) and at the microscopic material level (e.g., thermochemical interactions, slag chemistry, reduction kinetics, and surface behaviour). At the macroscopic level, we will analyse the system-wide effects of introducing variable bio-based reductants (including bioanthracite, enriched hydrogen gas, and process gases) across the BF–BOF, EAF, and DRI routes. Particular attention will be given to understanding how these inputs influence process thermodynamics, slag formation, gas flow dynamics, heat transfer, and plant energy balances. This will be supported by integrated mass/energy modelling to quantify operational trade-offs & identify process sensitivities under scaled and integrated configurations. At the microscopic level, we will conduct in-depth multi-scale characterisation of key material interactions using advanced analytical tools (e.g., spectroscopy, microscopy, compositional analysis) in order to gain an understanding into how the heterogeneous physical and chemical properties of the novel bio-based reductants interact with molten metal, slag, and refractory linings. This will include, e.g. characterisation of bioanthracite compacted with EAF slag and metal powder, analysing its reactivity, pH, mechanical stability, and carburising behaviour; assessment of catalytic slag materials from SiMn/FeMn processes used in methane cracking, including their role in gas-phase hydrogen enrichment and carbon deposition; exploration of gas cracking pathways for process gas rich in CH₄ and CO, identifying risks of catalyst degradation, or side reactions. These empirical insights feed into our project’s Quality-by-Design (QbD) framework and are used to refine the predictive models and control logic embedded in HoloSteel—our holonic, agent-based digital intelligence layer. HoloSteel integrates real-time data from sensors and characterisation tools to enable proactive detection, simulation, and mitigation of deviations or anomalies that might otherwise compromise energy performance, material yield, or product quality. By linking micro-level material behaviour with macro-level process control and by embedding this intelligence into a closed-loop decision-support system, we will build a robust foundation for managing complexity and ensuring stable, high-quality, low-CO₂ steel production under real industrial conditions.
Solve system-level issues within at least two low-CO2 production routes
In our project, we address system-level challenges across three strategic low-CO₂ production routes: green H₂-based DRI + bioC-based EAF; EAF and BF with biogenic anthracite; and Silico-manganese production with C–SiO₂-rich bioanthracite. In each route, we will tackle key system-level barriers (e.g. feedstock variability, energy efficiency, slag stability, process adaptability, and emissions control), by validating the controlled use of the biogenic reductants (bioanthracite and enriched hydrogen gas). Through a combination of pilot-scale validation (with our steelmaking partners AMMR, CELSA, ERA) and real-world operational data, we will ensure that: ■ Process performance remains stable despite the introduction of heterogeneous, low-cost feedstocks; ■ Energy balances are optimised via integration with site waste heat and gas flows; ■ Slag and gas behaviours are controlled through co-compaction strategies, catalytic effects, and tailored chemistry; and, ■ Digital process control is enabled via the HoloSteel intelligence layer, whereby it uses PAT for real-time monitoring and its Holonics Agent-based DT for predictive simulation and QbD principles will support stable, adaptive, and low-emission operations across all three routes. Such a comprehensive systems-level approach will ensure that the new biogenic reductants are effectively embedded into the full production environment, with adjustments to upstream preparation, downstream injection, digital control, and plant integration strategies. This will result in a series of validated decarbonised steelmaking pathways that are technically feasible, industrially scalable, and digitally enabled, and ready for further deployment.
Define solutions and provide concepts to address possible modifications or material substitutions in innovative installations for low CO2 iron and steel production
The biorefinery pathways developed by us enable the substitution of fossil-based reductants and fluxes with renewable, biogenic alternatives derived from biowastes. We define and validate solutions for integrating bioanthracite and green hydrogen into existing BF–BOF, EAF, and DRI steelmaking routes by proposing tailored material pretreatment strategies, such as fast HTC and chemical leaching, that aim to ensure consistent quality and compatibility with metallurgical requirements. These material substitutions will be supported by specific process modifications, such as co-compaction with EAF slag, catalytic cracking using Mn-rich residues, and adjustment of pH and density for optimal injection behaviour. Moreover, to ensure stable operation and product quality under these modified conditions, we integrate a holonic DT (HoloSteel) with advanced PAT for real-time process monitoring and improved process understanding and QbD principles. As such, this digital layer supports real-time monitoring, predictive simulation, and adaptive control of process parameters in response to variability in feedstock and product properties. Importantly, our technological solutions are designed for compatibility with both greenfield and brownfield steelmaking contexts to ensure that the innovations can be deployed in newly built plants as well as retrofitted into existing installations, for supporting widespread decarbonisation of the EU steel industry.
